title: It's too expensive
what_they_mean: They're worried about fitting another monthly payment into their budget, or they don't see the value relative to the cost.
rebuttal_strategies: Always agree and empathize with the objection first. Never rebut right away. This lowers the customer's guard.
Break down cost to daily amount (less than coffee)
Compare to other monthly expenses they don't question
Emphasize that premiums never increase
Show cost of funeral vs. premium payments over time
Offer different coverage amounts to fit budget
Use the 'cost of waiting' calculation

sample_responses.response: I understand price is important. Let's look at this investment—for about $2.50 per day, you can ensure your family has $15,000 for your final expenses. That's less than most people spend on coffee, but it guarantees your family won't face a $10,000+ bill when they're grieving.

You're right to be budget-conscious. Let me ask you this: what would be easier for you—finding $85 per month now, or asking your family to find $10,000 all at once when they're dealing with grief? Most of my clients tell me that once they set up automatic payments, they don't even notice it.

I hear this concern often. When you consider that the average funeral costs $9,000 and this guarantees $15,000 for less than $100 monthly, you're actually getting tremendous value. Even if you pay premiums for 20 years, your family still receives more than you paid in.

proof_elements: Funeral cost vs. premium investment chart
variations: I can't afford that right now
That's more than I want to spend
I'm on a fixed income
I need to think about the cost
That seems like a lot of money

title: I need to talk to my spouse/family
description: Delay tactic to avoid making immediate decision or genuine need for family input
what_they_mean: They either want to delay the decision, feel pressured, or genuinely believe family input is necessary for this decision.
emotional_drivers: Fear of making wrong decision alone
rebuttal_strategies: Always agree and empathize with the objection first. Never rebut right away. This lowers the customer's guard.
Acknowledge the importance of family input
Schedule a three-way call with spouse
Provide materials to share with family
Address the real concern behind the delay
Set a specific follow-up time
Create urgency around protection gap

sample_responses.response: That makes perfect sense—this affects your whole family. Would it be helpful if I called back when you're both available? I'm happy to explain everything to both of you and answer any questions your spouse might have.

I completely understand wanting to discuss this together. Let me ask you this: what questions do you think they'll have? I can make sure to address those concerns now, and then you'll have all the information you need for your discussion.

Family input is important for a decision like this. Here's what I'll do—let me email you a summary of everything we discussed so you have all the details to share with them. When would be a good time for me to follow up after you've had that conversation?

variations: I need to discuss this with my children
Let me run this by my financial advisor
I want to sleep on it
I never make decisions on the phone
We make financial decisions together

title: I already have life insurance
description: Belief that existing coverage is sufficient for final expenses
what_they_mean: They don't understand the difference between general life insurance and final expense coverage, or they assume all life insurance serves the same purpose.
rebuttal_strategies: Always agree and empathize with the objection first. Never rebut right away. This lowers the customer's guard.
Ask about their current coverage details
Explain the difference between term and final expense
Show how final expense complements existing coverage
Discuss what happens when term expires
Focus on immediate cash needs vs. long-term benefits
Address the specific purpose difference

sample_responses.response: That's great that you're already thinking about life insurance. Tell me about your current coverage—is it term or permanent? Most term policies are designed for income replacement while you're working, but final expense is specifically for immediate costs that come up right away when someone passes.

Existing life insurance is wonderful for your family's long-term needs. Final expense is different—it's specifically designed for immediate costs like funeral expenses that need to be paid right away, before any other insurance proceeds are available.

Work life insurance is a great benefit, but there are two things to consider: first, it usually ends when you retire, and second, it's typically not designed for final expenses. Final expense insurance is specifically for immediate costs your family faces in the first few weeks after you pass away.

proof_elements: Timeline of when different benefits pay
variations: My work provides life insurance
I have a big life insurance policy already
My term life covers everything
I don't need more insurance
I'm already paying for life insurance

title: I don't trust insurance companies
description: General skepticism about insurance industry reliability and claim payment
what_they_mean: They've had bad experiences with insurance companies, heard negative stories, or have general skepticism about the industry's reliability.
emotional_drivers: Fear of being taken advantage of
rebuttal_strategies: Always agree and empathize with the objection first. Never rebut right away. This lowers the customer's guard.
Acknowledge their concerns as valid
Share company ratings and stability information
Explain regulatory protections and guarantees
Use testimonials and success stories
Focus on specific company reputation
Discuss state guarantee funds

sample_responses.response: I understand that concern completely—trust is everything in this business. Let me share some information about our company's track record. We've been paying claims for over 75 years and have an A+ rating with AM Best, which is the gold standard for insurance company ratings.

You're smart to be cautious about claims. Here's what's helped other clients feel more confident: our state has a guaranty fund that protects policyholders, and final expense claims are actually the easiest claims insurance companies handle. There's rarely any investigation or dispute because the amounts are smaller and the purpose is clear.

I'm sorry you had a bad experience before. That's exactly why I work with this particular company—they have a reputation for straightforward policies and fast claim payment. Would it help if I showed you some recent testimonials from families who received their benefits?

variations: Insurance companies don't pay claims
You'll find a way not to pay
Insurance is a scam
I've been burned before
How do I know you'll be around?

title: I'm healthy - I don't need this yet
description: Belief that insurance isn't needed while in good health
what_they_mean: They don't see the urgency because they feel healthy now, or they don't understand that good health is actually the best time to get insurance.
rebuttal_strategies: Always agree and empathize with the objection first. Never rebut right away. This lowers the customer's guard.
Praise them for their good health
Explain that now is the best time to get coverage
Discuss how health can change unpredictably
Show benefits of applying while healthy
Use stories of people who waited too long
Create urgency around rate guarantees

sample_responses.response: That's wonderful that you're in good health! That's actually the perfect time to get this coverage—while you can qualify for the best rates and immediate benefits. The challenge is that health can change quickly and unpredictably, and once it does, this protection becomes much harder to get.

Your good health is a blessing, and I hope it continues for many years. Here's what I've learned after helping hundreds of families: the people who benefit most from final expense insurance are those who get it while they're healthy. Many of my clients wish they had gotten coverage sooner when their health changed unexpectedly.

I understand feeling like there's plenty of time. Let me share something that might surprise you: most of my clients who apply are healthy when they get coverage. They're not buying it because they think something will happen soon—they're buying it because they're smart enough to protect their family while they can still qualify.

variations: I'm only [young age]
I have plenty of time for this
I'll worry about this when I'm older
My family has good genes
Nothing's going to happen to me soon

title: I want to leave money to my kids, not an insurance company
description: Concern that premiums are wasted if not used, preferring to save or invest for heirs.
what_they_mean: They want to maximize what their children inherit and worry insurance is a 'lost' expense if not claimed.
rebuttal_strategies: Always agree and empathize with the objection first. Never rebut right away. This lowers the customer's guard.
Explain insurance as a guaranteed legacy, not a gamble
Show how savings can be depleted by emergencies, but insurance is protected
Highlight immediate payout and no probate delays
Compare return on premium vs. savings interest
Discuss tax-free benefit to heirs

sample_responses.response: That’s a loving instinct. The difference is, savings can be used up or lost to emergencies, but insurance guarantees your family gets a lump sum, tax-free, exactly when they need it most—no matter what.
I understand wanting every dollar to go to your kids. With insurance, you’re turning a small monthly payment into a much larger gift—often more than you could save on your own.

proof_elements: Comparison of savings vs. insurance growth
Stories of families helped by insurance payout
variations: I'd rather put money in a savings account
I want my kids to get everything
Isn't insurance just a waste if I don't use it?
I'd rather invest for my family

title: I'm too old or sick to qualify
description: Belief that age or health disqualifies them from coverage.
what_they_mean: They assume their health or age makes them ineligible, or that premiums will be unaffordable.
rebuttal_strategies: Always agree and empathize with the objection first. Never rebut right away. This lowers the customer's guard.
Explain guaranteed-issue and simplified-issue policies
Share stories of clients with health issues who qualified
Clarify that no medical exam is needed for many plans
Show affordable options for all ages
Emphasize dignity and peace of mind

sample_responses.response: You’re not alone—many of my clients have health conditions. The good news is, there are plans designed specifically for people in your situation, with no medical exam and guaranteed acceptance.
Age is just a number here. Some of our most popular plans accept applicants up to age 85, and the process is quick and respectful—no exams, just a few health questions.

variations: I'm too old for this
My health is too bad
No one will insure me
I have too many medical problems

title: I don't want to deal with medical exams
description: Objection to the hassle, discomfort, or fear of medical underwriting.
what_they_mean: They dislike needles, doctors, or paperwork, or fear being declined after an exam.
rebuttal_strategies: Always agree and empathize with the objection first. Never rebut right away. This lowers the customer's guard.
Highlight no-exam, instant-issue policies
Explain simple application process (just a few questions)
Share how most clients qualify in minutes
Emphasize privacy and convenience
Offer to walk through the process together

sample_responses.response: You’re in luck—most final expense plans don’t require any medical exam at all. Just a few health questions over the phone, and you can be covered in minutes.
I get it—no one likes paperwork or doctor visits. That’s why these plans are designed to be simple and fast, with no needles, no exams, and no hassle.

proof_elements: Comparison of exam vs. no-exam plans
variations: I hate needles
I don't want to see a doctor
I don't have time for all that paperwork
I just want something simple

title: I'd rather use my savings for final expenses
description: Belief that personal savings are a better way to cover funeral costs.
what_they_mean: They feel confident in their ability to save, or distrust insurance as unnecessary.
rebuttal_strategies: Always agree and empathize with the objection first. Never rebut right away. This lowers the customer's guard.
Explain how emergencies can deplete savings unexpectedly
Show how insurance guarantees funds regardless of timing
Compare growth of savings vs. insurance payout
Discuss tax-free, immediate payout to family
Share stories of families caught off guard by expenses

sample_responses.response: It’s great that you’re a planner. The challenge is, savings can be used up by emergencies or unexpected bills. Insurance guarantees your family gets the money, no matter what happens to your savings.

I understand wanting to keep things simple. The difference is, insurance multiplies your money—turning a small monthly payment into a much larger, tax-free benefit for your family, right when they need it.

proof_elements: Comparison of savings vs. insurance payout
variations: I have enough in the bank
I'll just use my savings
Why pay for insurance if I can save?
I don't believe in insurance

title: I don't trust giving out my bank information
description: Worry about sharing sensitive financial info for premium payments.
what_they_mean: They fear fraud, identity theft, or losing control over their finances.
rebuttal_strategies: Always agree and empathize with the objection first. Never rebut right away. This lowers the customer's guard.
Explain secure payment processing and privacy policies
Offer alternative payment methods (debit card, bill pay)
Share company credentials and regulatory oversight
Provide written documentation of security measures
Acknowledge and validate their caution

sample_responses.response: That’s a smart concern. We use secure, encrypted systems and never share your information. You can also use a debit card or set up bill pay if you prefer—whatever makes you most comfortable.
I understand—trust is earned. Our company is regulated and insured, and I can provide documentation on how your information is protected.

variations: I don't give out my bank info
Is this safe?
Can I pay another way?
I had my identity stolen before
